1: Boaz is introduced with the phrase ʾîš gibbôr ḥayil — 'a man of great standing/valor/substance.' Gibbôr ḥayil is a military-cultural designation used for elite warriors (1 Sam 9:1; 2 Kgs 15:20) but also for men of property, influence, and social standing. The same adjective root is applied to Ruth herself in 3:11 ('woman of ḥayil') — the pairing is significant: they are matched in character and quality. Boaz's name may derive from the same root ('in him is strength') or may be a place name. Matthew 1:5 names his mother as Rahab the Canaanite — if this is accurate, Boaz himself is the son of a Gentile convert woman, making his recognition of Ruth's faithfulness (2:11-12) personally resonant.
2: Ruth's proposal to glean invokes the Deuteronomic-Levitical gleaning laws: Lev 19:9-10 ('When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right up to its edge... you shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner'), Deut 24:19-21. The provision specifically covered three categories: the poor (ʿānî), the sojourner (gēr), the orphan (yātôm), and the widow (ʾalmānāh) — Ruth qualifies on at least three counts (poor, sojourner, widow). The practice was regulated but dependent on landowner compliance; a hostile landowner could make gleaning difficult or dangerous. Ruth's request for permission ('in whose eyes I find favor') reflects the practical reality that she needed the goodwill of the field's owner.
3: The narrator's phrase miqrehāh qārāh — usually translated 'as it happened' or 'her luck came upon her' — uses the word miqreh (H4745), which means 'chance occurrence,' 'what happens by lot,' or 'lot/fate.' The word appears in Ecclesiastes for the fate that comes equally to all (Eccl 2:14-15; 9:11). Here the narrator is being simultaneously realistic (she didn't know whose field it was) and theologically ironic (the reader can see YHWH's guiding hand). This 'coincidence' places Ruth in Boaz's field rather than another owner's — an 'accident' that will resolve the entire crisis. The narrative uses the language of chance to describe what is, from the reader's perspective, providential appointment.
4: Boaz's greeting 'YHWH be with you' (YHWH ʿimmākem) and the workers' response 'YHWH bless you' (yĕbārekeḵā YHWH) are covenant-embedded agricultural greetings. The presence of divine-name invocations in everyday work speech reflects the covenant community's integration of faith and daily life — YHWH's name belongs in the fields, not only in the sanctuary. The contrast with the Canaanite agricultural world (where Baal's blessing was invoked for crops) is implicit: Boaz's workers invoke YHWH's blessing over their labor. Ps 129:8 preserves a similar formula as a standard blessing for harvest workers, suggesting these were conventional greetings in Israelite agricultural communities.
5: Boaz's immediate question about Ruth ('Whose young woman is this?') demonstrates his attentiveness to his workers. The foreman's answer identifies Ruth by three markers: she is a Moabite woman, she returned with Naomi from Moab, and she asked permission before beginning to glean. The question and answer establish that Boaz has not met Ruth before — making his subsequent generosity an act of pure hesed based on reported character, not personal acquaintance. In a small-town (Bethlehem had perhaps 300-500 people at this period) this kind of report would travel fast; Boaz likely already knew about Ruth's arrival with Naomi (4:1-2 suggests the whole town knew).
6: The foreman identifies Ruth as 'the Moabite young woman who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab.' The identification is not pejorative but factual; it establishes Ruth's status as a gēr (resident alien) with the attendant legal protections and social distinctiveness. The foreman's subsequent report (v7) is favorable to Ruth — she asked properly, she worked hard. The gleaning system depended on both legal framework and social enforcement; a dishonest gleaner could take more than the prescribed portion, and a hostile foreman could make the work miserable. The foreman's positive portrayal of Ruth suggests she has already made a good impression on Boaz's workers.
7: Ruth's request to 'gather among the sheaves after the reapers' is more generous access than the law strictly required — gleaning was typically from what fell at the field edges, not from among the sheaves already gathered. Her asking ('please let me glean and gather among the sheaves') suggests she is requesting something slightly beyond the standard entitlement, and the foreman granted it. The note about the shelter (bêt here meaning perhaps a field shelter or booth for resting) and her brief rest demonstrates she worked continuously. The resting in the shelter would be in the heat of midday — a standard practice in the Levantine agricultural calendar when field labor paused during the hottest hours (approximately 11am-2pm).
8: Boaz's instruction 'Do not go to glean in another field or leave this one, but keep close to my young women' is protective as well as generous. The gleaning fields were not without risk for unaccompanied women — Boaz will explicitly warn his young men 'not to touch her' (v9, 15). The presence of foreign or unattached women in harvest fields could attract unwanted attention; Boaz's instruction to remain in his field and close to his female workers provides social protection through proximity and patronage. The protection of the vulnerable foreign woman through the social mechanisms of honor and patron-client relationships reflects actual Iron Age social practice.
9: Boaz tells Ruth to drink from the water that his young men have drawn — an act of genuine hospitality that reverses normal social expectations. In ancient agricultural settings, the lowest-status workers (often including women and foreigners) did not have access to the provisions of higher-status workers; water-drawing was itself a task assigned to low-status members of the household (Josh 9:21). Boaz gives Ruth access to water his young men drew — inverting the labor hierarchy. The same generosity appears in v14's meal invitation. ANE hospitality codes required care for guests; Boaz extends these codes to a foreign worker in his field who has no formal claim on his hospitality.
10: Ruth's response — 'Why have I found favor (ḥēn) in your eyes, that you should take notice of me, since I am a foreigner (nokrîyāh)?' — uses the stronger of the two Hebrew words for foreigner. Gēr (sojourner/resident alien) had legal protections in Israel; nokrî/nokrîyāh (foreigner/alien woman) was more ambiguous, sometimes implying someone outside the covenant community's obligations. Ruth uses the stronger, less favorable term for herself — emphasizing that Boaz's generosity has no legal or social obligation behind it. Her self-description as outside the patronage system she is benefiting from highlights the sheer grace of what Boaz is doing. The contrast is the setup for Boaz's explanation in v11-12.
11: Boaz's knowledge of Ruth's story demonstrates that information traveled through Bethlehem's small community: 'All that you have done for your mother-in-law since the death of your husband has been fully told to me.' The specific details he knows — how she left her father and mother and her homeland — echo the language of Abraham's call (Gen 12:1: 'go from your country and your kindred and your father's house'). Ruth's voluntary departure from her Moabite homeland parallels Abraham's departure from Mesopotamia; the narrator is establishing Ruth as a second Abraham — a Gentile called out of her land to follow YHWH. Boaz's recognition of this pattern in v12's blessing confirms that the parallel is intentional.
12: Boaz's prayer-blessing invokes YHWH as the one under whose 'wings' (kānāp̄ H3671) Ruth has come to take refuge. The same word kānāp̄ appears in Ruth 3:9 when Ruth asks Boaz to 'spread your wing over me' — creating an explicit link between YHWH's protection and Boaz's go'el action. The image of YHWH's protective wings recurs throughout the Psalms (17:8; 36:7; 57:1; 61:4; 63:7; 91:4) and in Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (Matt 23:37). Boaz's prayer points to YHWH as the ultimate source of Ruth's refuge; his own subsequent action will be the concrete means by which YHWH answers his own prayer. The blessing and the blessing's fulfillment will be accomplished through the same person.
13: Ruth's response — 'I have found favor (ḥēn) in your eyes, my lord, for you have comforted me and spoken kindly to your servant' — uses the vocabulary of grace and consolation. Ḥēn (favor/grace) is the gift of a superior to an inferior who has no claim on it; Ruth recognizes that Boaz's generosity is pure grace, not obligation. 'Spoken to the heart' (dibbartā ʿal-lēḇ) is the idiom for deeply comforting speech (Gen 34:3; Isa 40:2; Hos 2:14), used elsewhere for the speech that restores the despairing. Ruth's self-description as 'your servant' (šipḥāh) — the lowest female domestic rank — acknowledges the social distance while accepting the relationship Boaz has extended.
14: The meal invitation — 'Come here and eat some bread and dip your morsel in the wine' — is an act of genuine table fellowship. Ḥōmeṣ (translated 'wine' here) may refer to a vinegar-based dip or sour wine used as a condiment, common in harvest field meals. Boaz's serving Ruth himself ('he passed to her roasted grain') and her eating 'until she was satisfied and had some left over' evokes the abundance of divine provision — the same pattern as Elijah's jar of flour and cruse of oil that did not fail (1 Kgs 17:14-16) and Jesus's feeding of the multitude. The 'and she had some left over' is a small detail with large theological resonance: YHWH's provision through the hands of the hesed-actor exceeds the immediate need.
15: Boaz's private instruction to his workers — 'let her glean even among the sheaves' (not just the field edges) and 'pull out some stalks and leave them for her to glean, and do not rebuke her' — represents intentional, covert generosity. He is ensuring that Ruth's gleaning yields more than the legal minimum, but doing it in a way that preserves her dignity (she does not know it is staged provision). This combination of substantial help and dignity-preserving method reflects the highest form of ANE charity: making the provision look like natural good fortune rather than embarrassing charity. The Deuteronomic gleaning laws were a minimum floor; Boaz is going far beyond them while protecting Ruth's sense of having worked for what she received.
16: The instruction to 'pull out from the bundles' is a technical agricultural term — the reapers were to deliberately drop stalks from the already-gathered sheaves, ensuring Ruth's gleaning basket would be full. The practice of provisioning the poor through harvest abundance — ensuring the margins of the field were genuinely productive, not stripped — was a form of distribution built into the agricultural economy. The gleaning laws functioned as a kind of tax on the harvest in favor of the vulnerable. Boaz's exceeding of this minimum reflects the spirit-of-the-law interpretation that Jesus will later articulate as the goal of all law: love for neighbor that exceeds formal obligation.
17: An ephah (H374) of barley is approximately 22 kg (about 48 lbs) or roughly 29 liters — a substantial quantity for a single day's gleaning. The legal gleaning provision was meant to provide subsistence; an ephah far exceeds what a gleaner could normally expect. The quantity signals Boaz's secret generosity (vv15-16) has been effective. Naomi's amazement in v19 — 'Where did you glean today?' — is precisely this response: the amount is so large it requires an explanation. The ephah becomes the visible sign that YHWH is at work through a specific human agent, pointing Naomi toward the revelation of Boaz's name in v20.
18: Ruth carries the gleaned barley back to town and shows Naomi 'what she had gleaned' as well as 'what she had left over' from the meal (v14). The double provision — the gleaned grain and the leftover food — sets up Naomi's series of questions. The movement from the field to the town in the evening mirrors the daily rhythm of agricultural labor: work in the fields during the day, return to the settled community at night. The provision Ruth brings Naomi is substantial enough that Naomi can immediately see something unusual has happened. In a subsistence economy, an ephah of barley arriving in a widow's home was a significant event.
19: Naomi's three-part response to Ruth's account — 'Where did you glean today?', 'Where did you work?', and 'May he who took notice of you be blessed!' — demonstrates shrewd social reading. She immediately understands that the quantity of grain and the leftovers indicate specific, intentional favor from a named person; the provision was not random. Her blessing of 'the man who took notice of you' is invoked before she knows who it is — she blesses YHWH's agent before identifying him, which reflects the theological principle that the blessing belongs first to YHWH who sent the agent. The revelation of Boaz's name in v20 then allows the blessing to be personal and specific.
20: Naomi's exclamation on hearing Boaz's name — 'He has not abandoned his hesed to the living and to the dead' — identifies YHWH as the ultimate actor of hesed, acting through Boaz. The phrase 'to the living and to the dead' is legally significant: the go'el obligation includes perpetuating the dead man's name and inheritance (Deut 25:6-7; cf. 4:10). Naomi's declaration that Boaz is 'our close relative, one of our go'ēlîm' is the legal identification that frames the entire rest of the book: the go'el institution (kinsman-redeemer) is the legal mechanism through which the reversal of Naomi's condition will be accomplished. See Lev 25:25, 47-55 for the full legal framework.
21: Ruth reports Boaz's instruction to stay close to his workers until the end of all his harvest — both barley (April-May) and wheat (May-June). The invitation to remain throughout the entire harvest season provides Ruth with approximately two months of protected gleaning with exceptional yields. In practical terms, two months of gleaning at Boaz's rate would provide Naomi and Ruth with substantial food reserves. The arrangement is also social: Ruth's repeated presence in Boaz's field, under his protection and known to his workers, establishes her reputation in Bethlehem. By harvest's end, everyone in the town will know Ruth the Moabite as a woman of ḥayil (3:11: 'All my townspeople know you are a woman of valor').
22: Naomi's instruction 'go out with his young women' — not the young men — is a prudent social caution. Young unattached women working in harvest fields were at some risk from young male workers; staying close to Boaz's female workers (and under his protection) minimized that risk. The practice of women working together in gender-grouped agricultural tasks is attested in the archaeological record of Iron Age Israelite villages. The organization of labor by gender and social status reflects the community structures of small Iron Age villages where social roles were well-defined and departure from convention could create friction or danger.
23: Ruth gleans 'until the end of the barley and wheat harvests' — approximately April through June — then stays with Naomi. The gap between the wheat harvest and the next agricultural event (the threshing, ch3) would have been a few weeks. The barley harvest was associated with Passover (Lev 23:10-14); the wheat harvest with Pentecost/Shavuot (Lev 23:15-21). The entire story of Ruth takes place within the liturgical calendar of Israel's major spring feasts — a detail that links Ruth's gleaning and Boaz's redemption to the covenant community's celebration of YHWH's provision.
